title: DAVID — Eleanor of Aquitaine: Two Crowns, One Tongue
logline: Biography of Eleanor of Aquitaine with reconstructed Anglo-Norman court petition line; sources on file.

brand.title: DAVID · The Archive
brand.subtitle: Royal Tongues — dead languages and the voices of history
brand.cta: Follow the Archive — one attestation at a time. DAVID.

sources.citation: Roger of Hoveden, Gesta Regis Ricardi (1189) — Eleanor's release and role at Richard's coronation
sources.notes: Latin chronicle tradition; English translation in Fordham Medieval Sourcebook

sources.citation: Gerald of Wales, De principis instructione (c. 1195–1198) — Angevin court and Eleanor's influence
sources.notes: Contemporary moral-political commentary on Angevin dynasty

sources.citation: Marie de France, Lais (c. 1170–1200) — Anglo-Norman literary corpus contemporary with Eleanor's later career
sources.notes: Period language register reference; not Eleanor's own words

beats.speech_text: I am DAVID. Tonight, Eleanor of Aquitaine — duchess, queen of France, queen of England, and the woman who outlived two crowns.
Born around eleven twenty-two, she inherited Aquitaine — a third of France — at fifteen. Two marriages, two kings, five sons who would fight each other for thrones.
Her world spoke Latin for law, Old French and Anglo-Norman at court, and Occitan in the south. Power traveled in charters, crusades, and the letters that survived her.
Beaus sire, pur la charité de Dieu, aidez à mon fil Richard — reconstructed Anglo-Norman petition, echoing her documented intercession when Richard was captive in eleven ninety-three.
beats.speech_ipa: /bɔːs ˈsiːr pyr la ʃaʁite də djø ɛde a mɔ̃ fis ʁiˈʃar/

beats.speech_text: She died at Fontevraud in twelve oh-four — effigy still on stone, power still debated. What survives is not one voice, but the archive of what others wrote about her.

lines: PERIOD LINE: Reconstructed Anglo-Norman — petition register, not a verbatim attested quotation
METHOD: Chronicle evidence + contemporary Anglo-Norman literary corpus
